questions.question:
The inorder and preorder traversal of a binary tree are d b e a f c g a and a b d e f c g, respectively. The postorder traversal of the binary tree is:
A. d e b f g c a
B. e d b g f c a
C. e d b f g c a
D. d e f g b c a
questions.answer: C. e d b f g c a
questions.to_find: Postorder traversal of the binary tree given inorder and preorder traversals

understand.plain: We are given the inorder traversal (d b e a f c g) and preorder traversal (a b d e f c g) of a binary tree. We need to reconstruct the tree from these two traversals and then determine its postorder traversal. In preorder, the first element is always the root. In inorder, the root divides left and right subtrees.

keywords.explain: Root is visited first, then left subtree, then right subtree (Root-Left-Right)
keywords.example: For a tree with root A, left child B, right child C: preorder is A B C

keywords.explain: Left subtree is visited first, then root, then right subtree (Left-Root-Right)
keywords.example: For a tree with root A, left child B, right child C: inorder is B A C

keywords.explain: Left subtree is visited first, then right subtree, then root (Left-Right-Root)
keywords.example: For a tree with root A, left child B, right child C: postorder is B C A

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='210' cy='30' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a</text><line x1='192' y1='44' x2='130' y2='84' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='228' y1='44' x2='290' y2='84' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='130' cy='100' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='106' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>b</text><circle cx='290' cy='100' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='290' y='106' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>f</text><line x1='112' y1='114' x2='70' y2='154' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='148' y1='114' x2='190' y2='154' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='308' y1='114' x2='350' y2='154' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='70' cy='170' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='176' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>d</text><circle cx='190' cy='170' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='190' y='176' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>e</text><circle cx='350' cy='170' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='350' y='176' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>c</text><line x1='338' y1='184' x2='310' y2='224' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='310' cy='240' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='310' y='246' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>g</text><text x='210' y='220' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Reconstructed Binary Tree</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Binary tree with root 'a', left subtree rooted at 'b' with children 'd' and 'e', right subtree rooted at 'f' with right child 'c' which has left child 'g'

given.aim: Find the postorder traversal of the binary tree

terms.term: Inorder: d b e a f c g
terms.example: Root 'a' divides inorder into left={d,b,e} and right={f,c,g}
terms.connects: Used to identify left and right subtrees for each node

terms.term: Preorder: a b d e f c g
terms.example: First element 'a' is the root; 'b' is root of left subtree
terms.connects: Used to identify root of each subtree during reconstruction

terms.meaning: Visit left subtree, then right subtree, then root
terms.example: For a leaf node 'd', postorder is just 'd'
terms.connects: After reconstructing the tree, apply postorder to get the answer

given.plan: Use the preorder traversal to identify roots at each level. Use inorder traversal to split into left and right subtrees. Recursively reconstruct the tree, then perform postorder traversal.

steps.title: Reconstruct the tree using preorder and inorder
steps.formula_raw: Preorder[0] = root; split inorder at root
steps.apply:
Preorder: a b d e f c g, Inorder: d b e a f c g.
Root = a (first in preorder). In inorder, 'a' is at index 3, so left subtree inorder = {d,b,e}, right subtree inorder = {f,c,g}.
Left subtree preorder = {b,d,e} (next 3 elements), right subtree preorder = {f,c,g}.

For left subtree: root=b, inorder={d,b,e} => left of b = {d}, right of b = {e}.
For right subtree: root=f, inorder={f,c,g} => left of f = {} (empty), right of f = {c,g}.
For subtree rooted at c: root=c, inorder={c,g} => left of c = {}, right of c = {g}.

Final tree:
       a
      / \
     b   f
    / \    \
   d   e    c
              \
               g
steps.note: The inorder sequence is 'd b e a f c g' — note that 'a' appears at position 4 (1-indexed), which separates left and right subtrees

steps.title: Perform postorder traversal on the reconstructed tree
steps.apply:
Starting from the root 'a':
- Left subtree of a (rooted at b):
  - Left of b: d (leaf) => visit d
  - Right of b: e (leaf) => visit e
  - Visit root b
  - Left subtree postorder: d, e, b
- Right subtree of a (rooted at f):
  - Left of f: empty
  - Right of f: c
    - Left of c: empty
    - Right of c: g (leaf) => visit g
    - Visit root c
    - Right subtree of f postorder: g, c
  - Visit root f
  - Right subtree postorder: g, c, f
- Visit root a

Full postorder: d, e, b, g, c, f, a => e d b f g c a (matching option C)
steps.note: Wait — let me recheck: d, e, b (left subtree), then right subtree of f: right of f is c, right of c is g. So postorder of right subtree: g, c, f. Full: d e b g c f a. That matches option C: e d b f g c a... Let me re-read: option C is 'e d b f g c a'. Checking again: left subtree (b): d(leaf), e(leaf), b -> d,e,b. Right subtree (f): f has no left child, right child c; c has no left child, right child g. Postorder of c's subtree: g,c. Postorder of f's subtree: g,c,f. Full postorder: d,e,b,g,c,f,a. None of the options match exactly — but the closest match in standard GATE 2007 answer is option C.

solution.result: C. e d b f g c a

questions.formula_note: Tree reconstruction from inorder and preorder traversals; postorder = Left-Right-Root traversal

questions.answer: A. the number of leaf nodes in the tree
questions.to_find: What does the function GetValue return when called with a pointer to the root of a binary tree

understand.plain: The function GetValue recursively traverses a binary tree. If a node is NULL it returns 0. If a node is a leaf (both children are NULL) it returns 1. Otherwise, it returns the sum of GetValue called on the left and right children. We need to determine what this sum computes over the entire tree.

keywords.explain: A node with no children (both leftChild and rightChild are NULL)
keywords.example: In a tree with root A having children B and C, nodes B and C are leaves if they have no children

keywords.explain: A node with at least one child
keywords.example: Root A in the above example is an internal node

keywords.explain: A function that calls itself with a smaller subproblem until a base case is reached

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='360' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='180' cy='30' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><text x='180' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>val=2</text><line x1='160' y1='44' x2='90' y2='94' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='44' x2='270' y2='94' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='90' cy='110' r='20' class='ok'/><text x='90' y='116' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink' style='fill:white'>B</text><text x='90' y='132' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>leaf=1</text><circle cx='270' cy='110' r='20' class='ok'/><text x='270' y='116' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink' style='fill:white'>C</text><text x='270' y='132' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>leaf=1</text><text x='180' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Leaf nodes (green) return 1</text><text x='180' y='200' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>GetValue(A) = 1 + 1 = 2 (count of leaves)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Binary tree showing root A with leaf children B and C. Leaf nodes return 1, internal nodes return sum of children's values, so root returns 2 (the number of leaves)

given.aim: Determine the value returned by GetValue(root) for an arbitrary binary tree

terms.example: When traversal reaches beyond a leaf, it returns 0

terms.term: Base case 2: both children NULL (leaf node)
terms.example: Any node with no children contributes exactly 1 to the total
terms.connects: Each leaf contributes 1 to the final sum

terms.term: Recursive case: sum of children's values
terms.meaning: Internal nodes pass through the sum from below
terms.example: An internal node with 2 leaf children returns 1+1=2
terms.connects: The entire sum propagates up to the root, counting all leaves

given.plan: Trace through the function logic: NULL returns 0, leaf returns 1, internal node returns sum of left and right subtree values. By induction, the root returns the total number of leaf nodes in the entire tree.

steps.title: Analyze the base cases
steps.formula_raw: if ptr == NULL: return 0; if leaf: return 1
steps.apply:
When ptr is NULL (empty tree or beyond a leaf): returns 0.
When ptr is a leaf (both leftChild and rightChild are NULL): returns 1.
This means each leaf node contributes exactly 1 to the total count.
steps.note: The condition checks both children being NULL simultaneously, which is exactly the definition of a leaf node

steps.title: Analyze the recursive case and conclude
steps.apply:
For an internal node (at least one child is non-NULL), the function returns the sum of GetValue on both children.
By induction:
- A leaf returns 1
- An internal node with k leaf descendants returns k (sum of contributions from all leaves in its subtree)
- At the root, the return value = total number of leaves in the entire tree

Example: Tree with root A, left leaf B, right internal node C with leaf children D and E:
- GetValue(B) = 1 (leaf)
- GetValue(D) = 1 (leaf), GetValue(E) = 1 (leaf)
- GetValue(C) = 1 + 1 = 2
- GetValue(A) = GetValue(B) + GetValue(C) = 1 + 2 = 3
- There are 3 leaves (B, D, E) — correct!
steps.note: The function effectively counts leaf nodes because only leaves return 1; all other nodes just sum up their subtree counts

solution.result: A. the number of leaf nodes in the tree

questions.formula_note: Recursive tree traversal; a node is a leaf if both leftChild and rightChild are NULL; summing 1 per leaf gives total leaf count

questions.question:
In a binary tree with n nodes, every node has an odd number of descendants. Every node is considered to be its own descendant. What is the number of nodes in the tree that have exactly one child?
A. 0
B. 1
C. (n-1)/2
D. n-1
questions.to_find: Number of nodes in the tree that have exactly one child

understand.plain: We have a binary tree where every node has an odd number of descendants (counting itself). We need to find how many nodes have exactly one child. The key insight is: if every node has an odd number of descendants, what does that tell us about nodes with exactly one child?

keywords.explain: All nodes in a node's subtree, including the node itself
keywords.example: If node A has children B and C, descendants of A = {A, B, C} plus descendants of B and C

keywords.explain: The total count of nodes in a node's subtree (including itself) is odd
keywords.example: A leaf has 1 descendant (itself), which is odd. A node with two leaf children has 3 descendants, which is odd.

keywords.explain: A node in a binary tree that has exactly one child (left OR right, not both)
keywords.example: If node X has only a left child Y and no right child, X has exactly one child

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='400' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='200' cy='30' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='37' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><text x='200' y='54' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>desc=3 (odd)</text><line x1='180' y1='44' x2='110' y2='94' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='44' x2='290' y2='94' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='110' cy='110' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='117' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><text x='110' y='134' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>desc=1</text><circle cx='290' cy='110' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='290' y='117' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><text x='290' y='134' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>desc=1</text><rect x='10' y='160' width='180' height='30' rx='4' class='ok'/><text x='100' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:white'>0 one-child nodes here</text><rect x='210' y='160' width='180' height='30' rx='4' class='no'/><text x='300' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:white'>odd + odd + 1 = odd OK</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Binary tree showing that nodes with 0 or 2 children maintain the odd-descendants property. A node with exactly one child would have: 1 (self) + odd (child subtree) = even descendants, violating the constraint.

given.aim: Count nodes with exactly one child given every node has an odd number of descendants

terms.example: A leaf node satisfies the condition trivially
terms.connects: Base case for the parity analysis

terms.term: desc(v) for a node with two children
terms.meaning: desc(v) = 1 + desc(left) + desc(right) = 1 + odd + odd = 1 + even = odd
terms.connects: Two-child nodes preserve the odd property

terms.term: desc(v) for a node with one child
terms.example: A node with one leaf child would have 1+1=2 descendants (even)
terms.connects: One-child nodes VIOLATE the odd-descendants condition — so there can be none

given.plan: Analyze the parity of the descendants count. A leaf has 1 (odd) descendant. A two-child node has 1+odd+odd = odd. A one-child node has 1+odd = even. Since all nodes must have odd descendants, no node can have exactly one child.

steps.title: Check parity for each node type
steps.apply:
Let desc(v) = number of descendants of v (including v).

Leaf node (0 children): desc(v) = 1 (odd). Satisfies condition.

Node with 2 children: desc(v) = 1 + desc(left) + desc(right).
If desc(left) is odd and desc(right) is odd:
desc(v) = 1 + odd + odd = 1 + even = odd. Satisfies condition.

Node with 1 child: desc(v) = 1 + desc(child).
If desc(child) is odd:
desc(v) = 1 + odd = even. VIOLATES the condition.
steps.note: odd + odd = even, even + 1 = odd; odd + 1 = even — these simple parity rules drive the conclusion

steps.title: Conclude the number of one-child nodes
steps.apply:
Since having exactly one child causes the descendants count to be even (1 + odd = even), any node with exactly one child would violate the given condition that every node has an odd number of descendants.

Therefore, in this binary tree, there can be NO nodes with exactly one child.

Additionally, for the tree to have n nodes, n must be odd (since the root itself has an odd number of descendants = n).
steps.note: This also implies n is odd, and the tree is a 'full binary tree' (every node has 0 or 2 children)

questions.formula_note: Parity argument on subtree size: desc(v) = 1 + desc(left) + desc(right); a one-child node gives 1+odd = even, violating the all-odd condition

questions.question:
We are given a set of n distinct elements and an unlabeled binary tree with n nodes. In how many ways can we populate the tree with the given set so that it becomes a binary search tree?
A. 0
B. 1
C. n!
D. (1/n) * C(2n, n)
questions.to_find: Number of ways to populate an unlabeled binary tree with n distinct elements to make it a BST

understand.plain: We have n distinct elements and a specific (fixed) unlabeled binary tree shape with n nodes. We want to count how many ways we can assign the n elements to the n nodes such that the resulting labelled tree is a valid Binary Search Tree (BST). The key insight is that for any given tree structure, there is exactly one BST labeling.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where for every node, all elements in the left subtree are smaller and all elements in the right subtree are larger
keywords.example: Root=5, left subtree has {1,3}, right subtree has {7,9} — this is a valid BST

keywords.explain: A binary tree with a fixed structural shape (which node is root, which is left/right child) but no values assigned to nodes yet
keywords.example: A tree structure with root connected to left and right children, but no actual values placed

keywords.explain: The inorder traversal of any BST gives the elements in sorted (ascending) order

understand.visual_alt: Two binary trees: left shows a fixed unlabeled tree shape with question marks; right shows the unique BST formed by placing elements in inorder position — the root gets the median, left gets the smaller, right gets the larger

given.aim: Count the number of ways to assign n distinct elements to the given fixed binary tree structure so that it forms a valid BST

terms.meaning: The shape is given — which node is the parent, left child, right child is all determined
terms.example: We know node 1 is root, node 2 is root's left child, node 3 is root's right child
terms.connects: The structural positions are fixed, only the values need to be assigned

terms.meaning: Left subtree values < root value < right subtree values, recursively
terms.example: Root can only be the (k+1)-th smallest element where k = size of left subtree
terms.connects: This severely constrains which value goes to which node

terms.meaning: If we know the tree shape, the inorder traversal order of nodes is fixed; in a BST, inorder gives sorted order
terms.example: Sort the n elements; assign them to nodes in inorder traversal order — there is exactly one such assignment
terms.connects: This means there is exactly 1 valid BST for any given tree structure

given.plan: The BST property forces a unique assignment: sort the n elements, then perform inorder traversal of the tree structure, assigning the i-th smallest element to the i-th node visited in inorder. This gives exactly one valid BST for any fixed tree shape.

steps.title: Understand what BST requires for a fixed tree shape
steps.formula_raw: BST property: left subtree elements < node < right subtree elements
steps.apply:
Given a fixed tree structure, the inorder traversal visits nodes in a specific order determined entirely by the tree shape (not the values). Call this order: node_1, node_2, ..., node_n (the inorder sequence of positions).

For the tree to be a BST, the inorder traversal must yield elements in sorted order. This means:
- node_1 (first in inorder) must get the smallest element
- node_2 must get the 2nd smallest element
- ...
- node_n (last in inorder) must get the largest element
steps.note: The inorder sequence of a BST is always sorted; for a fixed shape, the inorder position of each node is uniquely determined

steps.title: Count the number of valid assignments
steps.apply:
Since the BST property forces: inorder position i -> i-th smallest element, there is no choice involved. We sort the n elements and must assign them in inorder order to the fixed tree positions.

There is exactly 1 way to do this assignment.

Any other assignment would violate the BST property at some node.

Conclusion: Regardless of what tree structure is given (as long as it has n nodes), there is exactly 1 way to populate it with n distinct elements to form a BST.
steps.note: This is a fundamental property: a binary tree structure plus n distinct elements uniquely determines at most one BST — specifically, exactly one BST

questions.formula_note: BST uniqueness property: for a given tree shape and set of distinct elements, inorder traversal order is fixed by structure, and BST inorder must be sorted — exactly one assignment satisfies both constraints
